explanation:
The sentence says that whenever a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must forward the message to the Multiple Interface Association Set, and then this component must perform an update. 'For every DM_i' means this should hold globally, for any instance. We will focus on atomic propositions: 'tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' (TC message sent), 'msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set' (message forwarded), and 'update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set' (update performed). To specify the sequence, we encode that: whenever tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node happens, then in the future, first the message is forwarded to the Multiple Interface Association component, and after that, an update is performed. In LTL, this can be captured as: Globally, if tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node holds, then eventually (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set holds and after that eventually update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set holds). For more precision, we encode that after the TC message is sent, the forwarding happens before the update, by requiring that after the message is sent, the message is eventually forwarded to the association set, and before that, no update happens. Then after this forwarding, eventually there is the update. So:
G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F(msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F(update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set))))

Alternatively, to ensure ordering, sometimes people use 'until', but with only atomic LTL we can encode this as above.

explanation_dict.natural_language: a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node
the message is forwarded to Multiple Interface Association Set
an update is performed by Multiple Interface Association Set
if a TC message is sent, then forwarding and update happen in order (eventually, with update possibly after forwarding)
globally (for every DM_i, at every time)

explanation: The property describes a sequence triggered by the atomic proposition 'tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node': if a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, then (1) the Local Node must forward the message to the Multiple Interface Association Set, and (2) this component must perform an update, in that order. Since this should happen for every occurrence of 'tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node', we use the globally (G) operator. We express the sequence by stating that after each occurrence of 'tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node', eventually (F) it is followed by 'msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set', and after that, eventually, 'update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set'. To ensure the order, we use the F (finally/eventually) operator twice, nesting the conditions: after the TC message, the forwarding must happen, and then after forwarding, the update must happen. The overall structure is: for all time (G), if 'tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' happens, then eventually message is forwarded to the Multiple Interface Association Set, and after that, eventually, the update is performed.

message forwarded to Multiple Interface Association Set
update performed by Multiple Interface Association Set

After forwarding, eventually perform update

For every occurrence of TC message, this happens globally

explanation: The property says: whenever a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node (tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node), the Local Node must forward the message to the Multiple Interface Association Set component (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set), and in turn, the Multiple Interface Association Set component must perform an update (update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set). This property must hold for every DM_i, which in LTL is naturally written with a G (globally, for all behaviors). 'In turn' means that after forwarding, the update must eventually occur. In LTL, we use implication (->) to say that if the sending event occurs, the two results (forwarding and update) must eventually follow, in order. The formula becomes: whenever tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node happens, eventually msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set must happen, and eventually after that, update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set must happen. This can be expressed as: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F (update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set)))) Note: for stricter sequencing (first forwarding, then update, both must occur after the TC message), the standard LTL idiom is to nest the Fs as above.

the message is forwarded to the Multiple Interface Association Set component
an update performed by this component

this property holds globally (for every TC message sent this must be true)

explanation: First, 'TC messages sent from DM_i to the Local Node component' maps to the atomic proposition tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node. The phrase 'this component must forward the message to the Multiple Interface Association Set component' means that, whenever tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node happens, eventually msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set must happen. Further, 'which in turn must perform an update' adds that eventually update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set must happen after the forwarding occurred. Since this holds for every DM_i, it is a global property: always, whenever a TC message is sent from DM_i to Local Node, it is (eventually) forwarded to the Multiple Interface Association Set, and (eventually after that) an update occurs. This is encoded with G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set))). Here, we nest the Fs to reflect that the update must happen after the forwarding, not necessarily at the same moment, and that both events must eventually follow the TC message. If you want to strictly enforce the ordering so that update only after forwarding, you may write: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set)).

explanation: The property states: Whenever a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must forward this message to the Multiple Interface Association Set component, and this component must eventually perform an update. This must hold for every DM_i, which means globally (G). Thus, we express: If 'tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' occurs, then eventually (F) the message is forwarded to the Multiple Interface Association Set ('msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set'), and eventually (F) an update is performed by this component ('update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set'). To encode a sequence (forwarding to Multiple Interface Association Set then update), we use the until (U) operator: after the forwarding, the update eventually happens. Thus, after a TC message is sent, it must be that the message is forwarded to the Multiple Interface Association Set before an update is performed, and the update must happen eventually. We can write: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set)), or for stricter sequencing: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F (update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set))).

explanation: The sentence describes that whenever a TC message (tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node) is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the node must forward this message to the Multiple Interface Association Set component (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set), and this component must then perform an update (update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set). The property must hold for every DM_i, so we require this to be globally true for any occurrence ('every' corresponds to G globally). The behavior is: whenever a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, eventually the message is forwarded to the Multiple Interface Association Set, and eventually an update by that component happens. This can be represented by: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set)). The formula expresses that after every relevant message, both forwarding and updating eventually occur.

explanation: The property describes a sequence of actions that must occur for every instance (DM_i) when a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node. Specifically: (1) whenever tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node occurs, (2) the Local Node must forward the message to Multiple Interface Association Set (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set), and (3) Multiple Interface Association Set must perform an update (update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set). To capture the required causal chain, we specify that globally, whenever a TC message is sent from DM_i to Local Node, it is eventually followed by a forwarding to Multiple Interface Association Set, and that is eventually followed by an update performed by this component. Thus, for every occurrence of tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node, eventually both actions must happen in sequence. This is encoded as: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set))

explanation:
We are told that when a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must forward the message to the Multiple Interface Association Set, which must then perform an update. We must capture this causal chain in LTL. 'For every DM_i' tells us to express this as a general property, so it should be interpreted as 'globally, whenever a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node… the forwarding and update must occur.'

Step 1: 'TC message sent from DM_i to Local Node' → atomic proposition: tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node

Step 2: 'this component (Local Node) must forward the message to the Multiple Interface Association Set component' → atomic proposition: msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set

Step 3: 'which in turn must perform an update' → atomic proposition: update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set

Step 4: The chain of behaviors should happen for every DM_i, i.e., globally (G), and the forwarding and update must eventually follow any TC message being sent to the Local Node — the standard chain is enforced with implication and F (finally/eventually): G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set)). Here, as in LTL, to ensure update occurs after forwarding, we nest F inside F, or we can use sequencing as: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set))

Alternatively, we can require that, whenever a TC message is sent, eventually forwarding happens, and from that point, eventually an update happens (stronger sequencing):
G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F(update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set)))
Or step by step:
G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set)) &
G(msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set -> F(update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set))

But the single-formula variant is standard, so we stick to that.
Final formula: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F(update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set)))

explanation: First, the natural language states: when a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node, the Local Node must forward the message to the Multiple Interface Association Set, and then this component must perform an update. This requirement applies globally (for every DM_i), so we use the G (globally) operator. The phrase 'forward the message to the Multiple Interface Association Set' maps to msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set. 'Update performed' by that set maps to update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set. The sequence (after a TC message from DM_i to Local Node, first the message is forwarded, then an update is performed) is represented in LTL by a chain of F (finally), but we want to ensure the order: after tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node, first the forward happens, then (after that) the update happens. In LTL, this can be captured by: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F(update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set)))). This says: always, if a TC message is sent from DM_i to Local Node, then eventually the forward happens, and (after that) eventually the update happens. To ensure the update happens after the forward, we can use the Until operator. Specifically: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> (F (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set U update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set)))). This asserts that, after the TC, eventually both the forward will happen, and then the update will happen subsequently.

explanation: The statement describes a sequence of actions that should happen every time a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node: (1) Local Node must forward the TC message to the Multiple Interface Association Set component, and (2) the Multiple Interface Association Set component must perform an update. This requirement should apply for all occurrences (i.e., globally) where a TC message is sent from DM_i to the Local Node. To capture this: The atomic proposition 'tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node' refers to TC sent from DM_i to Local Node. 'msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set' refers to the correct forwarding, and 'update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set' signals the required update. For each occurrence of 'tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node', it must eventually (F) be followed by a forwarding (msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set), and eventually, after that, an update (update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set) must take place. Thus: whenever a TC message is sent, it is guaranteed that eventually it will be forwarded, and eventually after the forwarding an update will happen. Because the statement is for 'every' DM_i, we use a global G operator. The forwarding must happen after the message was sent, so G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set)), and after that forwarding, eventually the update must happen, so G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F(update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set))). The most precise formula encodes that after each send, the forwarding, and after the forwarding, the update, both occur eventually. Therefore, we nest the temporal operators: G(tc_msg_sent_dm_to_local_node -> F(msg_forwarded_to_multiple_interface_association_set & F(update_performed_by_multiple_interface_association_set))).
